_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P1 AI w domu (formatywna)
_meta.interaction: Suwaki promptu (kind: tune) — ćwiczenie formatywne, NIE scorowane do zaliczenia
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE i ogólne (zero realnych danych/PII). Moduł formatywny — bez progu zaliczenia i certyfikatu.

intro: The difference between “AI is no help to me” and “AI saves me an hour a day” usually comes down to how you ask. In this module you will learn the three simple ingredients of a good request — context, goal, format — and you will see that refining an answer in conversation works better than hunting for the “perfect” question. All with examples from home life.

screens.title: The three ingredients of a good request

blocks.text: Simply your request to the AI — a question or instruction written in everyday words. The more clearly you say who is asking, why, and what kind of answer you expect, the better the result you get.

items: CONTEXT — who and in what situation: “I am a parent of two kids, I cook simple things”.
GOAL — what you want to achieve: “I need a dinner plan for 5 days, up to 30 minutes of cooking”.
FORMAT — what the answer should look like: “List it as bullet points, with a shopping list at the end”.

blocks.title: Compare for yourself
blocks.text: “What is for dinner?” will give you random ideas. “I am a parent, my kids do not eat spicy food, I have 30 minutes — suggest 3 dinners in bullet points” will give you something you can actually use.

screens.title: Iterating — a conversation instead of the perfect question

blocks.text: You do not have to write a perfect request on the first try. A chat is a conversation: you get a first version, you say what to fix, you get a better one. Anna Sample compares it to a fitting room — the first option rarely fits perfectly, but after two adjustments it fits like a glove.

items: Write your request as best you can — no perfectionism.
Read the answer and name what bothers you: too long? too formal? not the right topic?
Ask for a fix in one sentence: “shorter”, “in a warmer tone”, “without difficult words”.
Repeat until it is right — usually 2-3 rounds are enough.

blocks.title: Refining is not failure
blocks.text: Following up and correcting is the normal way of working with AI — just like clarifying your order in a restaurant. The assistant will not take offense at your fifth correction.
See how the same three ingredients (context, goal, format) work in typical household matters. You can treat each “better request” as a template and swap in your own details.

rows: “Write a short, polite e-mail to the homeowners’ association (contact: zarzad@przyklad.test) asking them to repair the intercom. Matter-of-fact, no complaints.”
“I work until 4 p.m., the kids have swimming on Tuesday. Lay out a weekly plan of household chores in a table, 2 tasks per day.”
“I have eggs, pasta, spinach, and cheese. Suggest 2 quick dinners using only these ingredients, steps in bullet points.”
“I know English at a basic level. Every day I want 5 sentences about cooking with a translation and a simple quiz at the end.”
“I am planning a 3-day trip with a 7-year-old, up to 200 km from home, on a budget. Suggest 3 options with pros and cons in bullet points.”
blocks.textAlt: The table compares weak and better requests in five situations: an e-mail, a weekly plan, a recipe from the fridge, learning a language, and travel. The better requests always include context (who and in what situation), a goal (what exactly should be produced), and a format (bullet points, a table, a quiz).

blocks.title: Collect your favorites
blocks.text: When a request works brilliantly, save it in your notes (for example, in NotkaApp). After a month you have your own prompt cheat sheet for every occasion.

In a moment you will play with the “sliders” of a request: you will see how the same situation — asking for an e-mail to the neighbors — changes when you add context, set a format, and ask for plain language. There is no passing threshold here; the goal is to see with your own eyes that a clear request = a better answer.

blocks.title: The golden rule
blocks.text: If an answer disappointed you, 9 times out of 10 adding one sentence of context or specifying a format will help — before you conclude that “AI does not work”.

interaction.title: Tune your request
interaction.intro: The situation: you ask the AI for an e-mail to your neighbors about a noisy renovation. Move the sliders and watch how the answer changes. Then answer one checkpoint question.
interaction.outputLabel: What the AI answer looks like

levels.label: None
levels.effect: The AI guesses the situation: the e-mail comes out generic, may be too harsh or completely off topic — it does not know who is writing or to whom.

levels.label: Who and why
levels.effect: The AI knows you are writing as a neighbor, you care about good relations, and you only want to agree on quiet hours — the e-mail gets to the heart of the matter.

levels.label: Any
levels.effect: You get a long block of text — you have to shorten and organize it yourself before sending.

levels.label: Short, with bullet points
levels.effect: The e-mail has 3 sentences of introduction and concrete points (what the request is about, proposed hours) — ready to send almost without edits.

levels.label: Any
levels.effect: By default the AI writes in stiff, bureaucratic language (“With reference to the situation at hand…”) — it sounds like a letter from a government office, not a note from a neighbor.

levels.label: Plain, friendly language
levels.effect: The e-mail sounds natural and human — it politely names the problem and proposes a solution without souring the relationship with the neighbors.

checkpoint.prompt: Which combination of settings will give the best e-mail to the neighbors?

options.text: Context: none · Format: any · Tone: any — the AI knows best on its own.
Context: who and why · Format: short, with bullet points · Tone: plain, friendly language.
Context: who and why · Format: any · Tone: any — context alone is enough.
Context: none · Format: short, with bullet points · Tone: plain language — form matters more than substance.

checkpoint.feedbackCorrect: Exactly! Context tells the AI what it is really about, format organizes the content, and a plain tone takes care of the relationship. These three ingredients together produce an e-mail ready to send.
checkpoint.feedbackIncorrect: Almost! The full set works best: context (who is writing and why), format (short, in bullet points), and tone (plain, friendly language). Context alone helps, but without format and tone the e-mail still needs edits.

points: A good request has three ingredients: context (who and in what situation), a goal (what should be produced), and a format (what it should look like).
Do not hunt for the perfect prompt — refine the answer in conversation; 2-3 rounds are usually enough.
Household situations (an e-mail, a plan, a recipe, language learning, travel) have repeatable request patterns — collect the ones that work.
A disappointing answer is most often a sign that context or format was missing — not that “AI does not work”.
checklist: Does my request say who I am and why I am asking?
Did I specify what the answer should look like (bullet points, a table, length)?
Did I refine the answer instead of starting from scratch?
Did I save a request that worked brilliantly so I can use it again?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — a good request to AI
summary.nextStep: Next step: in the “AI in your phone and home” module you will see where AI already lives in your devices — and which of those features genuinely make life easier.
